programs.name: Medicaid for Seniors/Disabled
programs.tagline: Health coverage for your parent if they're 65+ or disabled with limited income and assets.

localEntities.name: Local DSS offices statewide; e.g., Arlington DHS Public Assistance.

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ or disabled and has limited income and assets, Virginia Medicaid can cover their medical care, prescriptions, and long-term care services. This program provides comprehensive health coverage including doctor visits, hospital care, nursing home care, and home-based care services. Income limits range from $1,084/month for full coverage to $2,982/month for community-based care programs, with asset limits typically $2,000 for individuals.

Unlike regular Medicaid, this program for aged, blind, and disabled (ABD) adults requires both income and asset tests. Your parent can keep their home and one car, but other savings and assets count toward the $2,000 limit. If their income is slightly over the limit, they may still qualify through a "spenddown" process using medical bills to reduce countable income.

otherRequirements: Must be disabled (SSA/SSI determination or Medicaid assessment) or blind if under 65
U.S. citizen, permanent resident, refugee, or asylee

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at Cover Virginia or through your local DSS office — processing takes 45-90 days.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect Social Security cards, income statements, bank statements, insurance cards, and immigration documents if applicable.

steps.title: Complete Cover Virginia Application
steps.description: Fill out the main application online at coverva.dmas.virginia.gov or get help at your local DSS office.

steps.title: Submit Appendix D form
steps.description: Complete the separate ABD (Aged, Blind, Disabled) form — this covers the additional asset and disability requirements.

steps.title: Attend disability assessment if needed
steps.description: If you're under 65 and not already receiving SSI, you may need a Medicaid Disability Unit evaluation.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible for home and community-based services due to limited slots
applicationGuide.tip: Apply even if you think your parent's income is slightly too high — the spenddown process may still qualify them using medical bills.

tiers.name: Full Coverage Medicaid
tiers.description: Complete health coverage including long-term care
tiers.coverage: All medical services, prescriptions, nursing home, home care

tiers.name: Limited Coverage Medicaid
tiers.coverage: Doctor visits, hospital care, prescriptions (no long-term care)

tiers.name: Community Based Care
tiers.description: Care hours based on need and income
tiers.coverage: Medical care plus home-based care services

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Life insurance with cash value over $1,500
excluded: Primary home where your parent lives
Life insurance with face value under $1,500

categories.name: Identity
items: Social Security card or proof of application
Immigration documents (if not U.S.-born): alien registration card, I-94, passport

categories.name: Financial
items: Pay stubs or Social Security award letters
Proof of any other income (pensions, investments)

categories.name: Medical
items: Proof of disability (SSI award letter or medical records)
Recent medical bills if applying for spenddown

contentSections.text: Home and community-based care services may have waitlists due to limited slots. Apply early if your parent needs these services.
Virginia recovers long-term care costs from estates after death. The state can place a lien on your parent's home if they receive nursing home or home care services.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify through 'Medically Needy' spenddown. Virginia calculates how much their income exceeds the limit, and if their monthly medical bills meet or exceed that amount, they can get coverage for the rest of the month.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home where your parent lives is exempt from asset limits. However, Virginia may recover long-term care costs from the home's value after death through estate recovery.

faqs.question: Does my parent automatically qualify if they get SSI?
faqs.answer: Not automatically in Virginia. They still need to apply through Cover Virginia and meet the income and asset tests, even with SSI benefits.

faqs.question: What's the difference between this and regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: This program tests both income and assets (regular Medicaid only tests income). It's specifically for people 65+ or disabled, with different income limits and coverage options including long-term care services.

faqs.question: How long does coverage last once approved?
faqs.answer: Coverage continues as long as your parent meets the requirements. They'll need to renew annually and report any changes in income or assets that might affect eligibility.

faqs.question: Can my parent work and still qualify?
faqs.answer: If your parent is disabled and under 65, they may qualify for 'Medicaid Works' which allows higher income for working individuals. People over 65 have more limited work income allowances.

programs.name: Virginia PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, PACE provides all medical services through one coordinated team.

localEntities.name: Specific sites listed in DMAS 'Virginia PACE Locations' document (addresses, phones, websites for each PACE organization across Virginia).

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and at risk of nursing home placement, Virginia PACE may cover all their medical care through one coordinated team — allowing them to stay in their community. Because PACE becomes your parent's sole Medicare and Medicaid provider, they won't need to coordinate between different doctors, specialists, or facilities.

PACE covers everything from primary care and prescription drugs to adult day care, physical therapy, and even nursing facility care if temporarily needed. Your parent must live in a PACE service area (not available statewide) and qualify medically for nursing facility level of care. Income limit: approximately $2,901/month for 2025. Asset limit: typically $2,000 (excluding home and one vehicle).

Only about 7% of PACE participants live in nursing facilities long-term — the program is designed to keep seniors in their communities with comprehensive support.

summary: Needs nursing facility level of care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified as requiring nursing facility level of care through a clinical assessment (UAI screening) showing your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management.
otherRequirements: Live in a PACE service area
Be enrolled in Medicare or Medicaid
Able to safely live in community with PACE support

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE provider directly — processing time varies by location and may include waitlists.

steps.title: Find your local PACE provider
steps.description: Check if PACE is available in your parent's area using the DMAS Virginia PACE Locations directory. Not all areas of Virginia have PACE.

steps.title: Contact the PACE center directly
steps.description: Call the specific PACE organization in your area. Each has its own phone number and application process.

steps.title: Request nursing facility level of care screening
steps.description: Your parent will need a formal clinical assessment (UAI screening) to determine if they qualify for nursing facility level of care.

steps.title: Complete financial eligibility review
steps.description: Provide income and asset documentation to determine Medicaid financial eligibility under Virginia's rules.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible regional waitlists vary by PACE site
applicationGuide.tip: Call your local PACE provider first to confirm availability in your area — this program is not statewide.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Cash value of life insurance over $1,500

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of address in PACE service area

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records showing need for daily care assistance

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in specific areas of Virginia. You must live in a PACE service area to qualify — check availability before starting the application process.
Once enrolled, PACE becomes your parent's sole provider for all Medicare and Medicaid services. They cannot use other doctors or facilities for covered services without PACE approval.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's primary home does not count toward the $2,000 asset limit. PACE is specifically designed to help seniors stay in their communities rather than move to nursing facilities.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the $2,901 limit?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify through Medicaid spend-down rules, where excess income can be applied to medical expenses. Contact your local PACE provider to discuss options — Virginia's Medicaid long-term care rules may allow higher income in some situations.

faqs.question: Can she still see her current doctors after joining PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE becomes the sole provider of all Medicare and Medicaid services. Your parent will need to use PACE's interdisciplinary team of doctors and specialists. However, this team coordinates all care, eliminating the need to manage multiple providers.

faqs.question: What happens if PACE isn't available in our area?
faqs.answer: PACE is not statewide in Virginia. If it's not available in your parent's area, look into Virginia's Medicaid waiver programs like the Elderly or Disabled with Consumer Direction (EDCD) waiver, which also helps seniors avoid nursing home placement.

faqs.question: How is this different from regular Medicare and Medicaid?
faqs.answer: PACE combines Medicare and Medicaid benefits into one coordinated program with additional services like adult day care and respite care. Instead of managing multiple providers, one interdisciplinary team handles all medical care, therapy, and support services.

faqs.question: Can she leave PACE if she doesn't like it?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE enrollment is voluntary and your parent can disenroll at any time. However, leaving PACE may affect her prior Medicare and Medicaid coverage, so discuss the implications with the PACE team before disenrolling.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: If your parent struggles with Medicare premiums and deductibles, they may qualify for help paying $1,000–4,000+ annually in Medicare costs.

localEntities.name: Local Department of Social Services offices (locator: dss.virginia.gov/localagency)

programs.intro:
If your parent is on Medicare and struggling with premiums, deductibles, or copays, three Virginia programs can help pay these costs. The Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB) program covers all Medicare cost-sharing including premiums, deductibles, and copays. The Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB) and Qualifying Individual (QI) programs cover Medicare Part B premiums only — saving roughly $1,980 per year in 2026.

These programs have different income limits: QMB for those earning up to about $1,350/month ($1,824/month for couples), SLMB up to roughly $1,620/month single, and QI up to about $1,823/month single. Because you qualify based on income level, your parent automatically gets Extra Help with prescription drug costs too.

You must apply through your local Virginia Department of Social Services office — there's no statewide online application. The QI program has limited federal funding and may have waitlists.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premiums plus deductibles and copays for QMB enrollees

otherRequirements: Must be entitled to Medicare Part A
Must have Medicare Part B for SLMB/QI programs

applicationGuide.summary: Apply at your local Virginia Department of Social Services office — typically takes 45 days to process.

steps.title: Find your local DSS office
steps.description: Use the office locator at dss.virginia.gov/localagency to find your parent's local Department of Social Services office.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income (Social Security awards, bank statements), Medicare card showing Parts A and B, and asset documentation.

steps.title: Complete the MEDS application
steps.description: Fill out the Medicaid application form (available at the office or download from dss.virginia.gov/benefit_programs/msp.html).

steps.title: Submit application in person or by mail
steps.description: Bring or mail the completed application and documents to your local DSS office.

applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program may have waitlists due to limited federal funding
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for QMB first if income qualifies — it provides the most comprehensive coverage and has priority over other programs.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: All Medicare Part A premiums, Part B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copays

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Medicare Part B premiums only (~$1,980/year savings)

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.description: Part B premium help with potential waitlist
tiers.coverage: Medicare Part B premiums only (~$1,980/year savings)

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Cash value of life insurance over $1,500
excluded: Up to $3,500 set aside for burial expenses

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of Virginia residency (utility bill, lease)

categories.name: Medicare
items: Medicare card showing Part A and Part B coverage

contentSections.text: QI program has limited federal funding and uses a first-come, first-served waitlist. If your parent's income qualifies for multiple programs, apply for QMB or SLMB first as they have guaranteed funding.

Income and asset limits change every January with federal poverty level adjustments. If your parent was previously denied, they may qualify now with updated limits — contact your local DSS office to verify current figures.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and car if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home has no equity limit and one vehicle of any value is completely exempt from asset counting.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the QMB limit?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify for SLMB (up to ~$1,620/month) or QI (up to ~$1,823/month) which both cover Medicare Part B premiums worth about $1,980/year. Virginia may also disregard certain types of income.

faqs.question: Will doctors still bill my parent for Medicare costs if they have QMB?
faqs.answer: No. Providers are prohibited from billing QMB enrollees for any Medicare cost-sharing. Your parent just needs to show their Medicaid card along with their Medicare card at appointments.

faqs.question: Does qualifying for this affect other benefits my parent receives?
faqs.answer: No, it typically helps. Your parent automatically qualifies for Extra Help with prescription drug costs and may become eligible for other programs. It doesn't reduce Social Security or other benefits.

faqs.question: How long does the application take and when do benefits start?
faqs.answer: Applications typically take 45 days to process. If approved, benefits can be retroactive to the month of application, and Virginia will pay back any Medicare costs your parent paid during that time.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income changes after they're approved?
faqs.answer: Report income changes to your local DSS office promptly. Small increases may not affect eligibility, but large changes could. They'll reassess annually, and your parent may move between program tiers based on new income.
